name: Unnamed Location (Chalcedony/Curtisite)
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding site near San Francisco offering chalcedony and curtisite specimens. Free collecting with a 25 lb daily limit on gentle terrain at low elevation.
description: This unnamed BLM location in the San Francisco Bay area provides an accessible opportunity to collect chalcedony and curtisite specimens without a permit. The site sits at only 139 feet elevation with gentle slopes, making it suitable for rockhounders of various experience levels. The terrain is relatively open, allowing collectors to search hillsides and washes for quality microcrystalline quartz specimens. As a public BLM site, this location is free to visit and collect from, subject to standard personal use regulations. The proximity to the Bay Area makes this a convenient destination for local rockhounding enthusiasts looking to avoid long drives while still accessing productive mineral sites.
history: The chalcedony and curtisite deposits in the San Francisco Bay region formed during the Cenozoic era through silica-rich hydrothermal activity associated with the Coast Ranges geological province. These microcrystalline quartz minerals precipitated from groundwater solutions flowing through fractures in metamorphic and sedimentary rocks, creating nodules and veins that weathered out into accessible collecting zones over millions of years.
tips: Search the gentle hillsides and small washes where erosion has exposed chalcedony nodules and curtisite fragments. Bring a small 5-pound hammer and chisel to extract specimens from weathered outcrops; look for translucent to opaque chalcedony in white, gray, and tan colors. The low elevation means this site is productive year-round, but avoid collecting immediately after heavy rain when muddy slopes can become slippery.
terrain_notes: Elevation 139 feet with gentle slopes throughout. Terrain consists of open hillside and light wash access, relatively easy walking. No significant vegetation obstacles. Expect soft soil and decomposed rock underfoot. Parking area likely involves a pullout or informal lot near the collection zone. Footing is stable on the slopes, though post-rain conditions may create muddy patches.
written_directions: From downtown San Francisco, drive south or west toward the Bay area rockhounding zone near the given coordinates (37.76977, -122.426949). Use GPS or mapping tools to navigate to the BLM land parcel. Access is likely via local roads from San Francisco or nearby communities. Contact the Bureau of Land Management California office or check BLM.gov for the most current road conditions and exact access points, as urban BLM parcels sometimes have access restrictions.
road_conditions: Likely improved dirt or paved roads to the site. Low elevation minimizes seasonal washout risk. Check with local BLM office for current conditions.
parking_notes: Informal pullout or small parking area near the collection zone. Limited capacity typical of BLM sites. Arrive early during weekends.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collecting is free and requires no permit on this BLM land. Respect any posted closure signs and avoid disturbing sensitive habitat.
rules:
Personal use only - no commercial collection

nearest_services: San Francisco - approximately 5-10 miles depending on exact access point. Full range of services including gas, food, water, and supplies available in the city.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers. Easy footing on hillsides and wash bottoms. No wheelchair access to the collection zone itself, but the low elevation and minimal grade changes make this suitable for families and those with limited mobility who can tolerate uneven ground. Sturdy walking shoes recommended.

faq.question: Where can I find chalcedony and curtisite near San Francisco without a rockhounding permit?
faq.answer: This unnamed BLM location near San Francisco offers free chalcedony and curtisite collecting under personal use regulations. No permit is required - simply follow the 25 lb daily limit and collect responsibly. The site is on public BLM land, making it one of the rare accessible rockhounding spots in the Bay Area.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect chalcedony specimens at this San Francisco area rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Bring a small 5-pound hammer and cold chisel to extract chalcedony nodules from weathered outcrops. A hand lens helps identify quality specimens. Pack a small collection bag or backpack to carry specimens - remember the 25 lb daily limit. Wear heavy gloves for protection from sharp rock fragments.

faq.question: Is this Bay Area rockhounding location beginner friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, this site is excellent for beginners. The terrain is easy with gentle slopes, no special permits are required, and chalcedony is straightforward to identify and collect. It's close to San Francisco, making it ideal for first-time rockhounders who want to avoid long drives while learning proper collecting techniques.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding chalcedony at this unnamed San Francisco location?
faq.answer: October through April is optimal. These months offer mild weather and lower rain risk compared to winter storms. The low elevation (139 feet) means this site is productive year-round, but post-rain conditions can create muddy slopes. Summer and early fall can be warm; plan early morning visits.

faq.question: Can I bring my dog rockhounding at this BLM location near San Francisco?
faq.answer: Yes, this is a dog-friendly site. The gentle terrain and open hillsides are suitable for dogs on leash. Bring plenty of water for both you and your pet, as the site has minimal shade at low elevation. Check current BLM regulations regarding pets to ensure compliance.

faq.question: How much chalcedony can I collect per day at this free San Francisco rockhounding site?
faq.answer: You can collect a maximum of 25 pounds per day under FLPMA personal use regulations for BLM land. This is a federal limit - no permits or fees apply. This amount is enough for most rockhounders to take home quality specimens without commercial intent.
